items.q: Is an issue automatically ethical rather than legal just because it involves computers?
items.correct: No - hacking and data misuse are LEGAL issues covered by specific laws, while questions the law does not settle, like algorithmic bias, are ethical issues.
items.misconception: Yes - all computing issues are ethical because technology is new.
items.random_wrong: Yes - laws cannot apply to anything done online.
items.why: Students blur the categories. Sort by test: could you be prosecuted (legal), or is it a question of right and wrong the law leaves open (ethical)?

items.correct: No - much e-waste ends up in landfill or is exported, where toxic materials like lead and mercury can harm people and the environment.
items.misconception: Yes - every old device handed in is fully and safely recycled.
items.random_wrong: Yes - e-waste is harmless because devices are switched off.
items.why: The recycling bin gives false comfort. Safe processing is expensive, so significant volumes are dumped or informally dismantled, releasing toxins.

items.q: Does data protection law mean companies can never share your data?
items.correct: No - data can be shared when there is a lawful basis, such as your consent or a contract; the law regulates sharing rather than banning it.
items.misconception: Yes - any sharing of personal data is automatically illegal.
items.random_wrong: Yes - companies may only share data with the government.
items.why: Students read protection as prohibition. The Act imposes principles - fairness, security, purpose limitation - within which lawful processing and sharing happen.

items.q: Discarded computers, phones and other electronic devices are known as:
items.why: Correct answer: E-waste. “Freeware” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: How organisations collect, store and use personal data
items.why: Correct answer: How organisations collect, store and use personal data. “The speed of broadband connections” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is an ENVIRONMENTAL impact of computing?
items.correct: The energy consumed by large data centres
items.why: It's The energy consumed by large data centres. “Cyberbullying on social media” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The Computer Misuse Act 1990 makes it illegal to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Access computer material without authorisation. “Sell second-hand computers” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the UK's current Data Protection Act passed?
items.why: The real figure is 2018. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
